1: Like the dark cloud that rested on the top of Sinai (Exodus 20:19), the veil separated the officiating priest who burned the daily incense and ministered in other ways in the Holy Place from the immediate presence of God (Exodus 40:26; Leviticus 4:6)… Moses, who at the invitation of the Lord passed through the veil of the cloud into the divine presence (see Exodus 24). (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie, Gospel Symbolism [Salt Lake City: Bookcraft, 1999], 109, 119.)
2: kingdom against kingdom [Septuagint has "nome against nome"; Egypt was divided into forty-two nomes or districts i.e. states].
Egyptians against the Egyptians [Lower against Upper north v.south]
3: charmers [soothsayers imitated the faint sound which was attributed to the spirits of the dead],
5: Egypt's economic structure was connected to the great Nile River. Isaiah may be speaking symbolically here and in the following five verses, referring to the economic collapse Egypt will suffer, or the prophecy might refer to an actual drought that will cause economic disintegration in Egypt.(Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1998], 179.)
7: Descriptive of the appearance of a river when its bottom is bare and its banks stripped of vegetation by long drought.
8: Isa. 19:8-9 speaks of three of Egypt's industries: fishing (casting the net and casting the hook), flax growing, and weaving. The demise of these three industries points to national economic disaster.
10: Jerusalem Bible reads: "The weavers will be dismayed and all the workmen dejected."
11: Zoan (Tanis), in the northern delta, was Egypt's capital from approximately 1100 to 660 B.C. and the location from which Moses sent forth the plagues of Egypt.
13: Officers and leaders of Zoan (19:11) and Noph (or Memphis, once Egypt's capital during the Old Kingdom), have lost their ability to lead. They act more like fools than true leaders, perhaps due to selfdeception.
15: The head (Egypt's leaders) and tail (the followers) will be unable to be productive in either the quality or the quantity of their work.
16: Isaiah's imagery presents a clear picture as he compares the fears of the Egyptians to a woman who demonstrates concern for her children during impending danger.
18: DSS Isa. Scroll corrects City of Destruction to City of the Sun
23: Israel is between them - Israel is the bridge between Egypt and Assyria. The only way out of the world is through Israel.
25: This brief prophecy concerning an event that occurred in Isaiah's lifetime adds detail to the burden of Egypt (Isa. 20:1; 19:1-15). When King Sargon of Assyria sent his military leader, Tartan, to take the former Philistine city of Ashdod, Isaiah dressed like a slave for three years to warn Israel that if Egypt and Ethiopia were unable to defend themselves against Assyrian enslavement, they certainly could not help protect Judah (Isa. 20:2). Within ten years Assyria indeed invaded Judah and besieged Jerusalem. Isaiah gave a detailed account later of how King Hezekiah of Judah did indeed depend upon the Lord and not upon "this bruised reed, even upon Egypt" (2 Kgs. 18:21; Isa. 20:6). (Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, 516.)
